refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported via telemetry.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a KPI requirement that must be operationalized with measurable acceptance criteria (p95 threshold), defined duty cycle context, and explicit telemetry linkage for verification. The refinement adds observability and context boundaries to make the requirement verifiable and traceable to system performance.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure actual cycle time across ≥100 representative load→travel→dump cycles under agreed duty cycle conditions; compute p95 percentile; compare against CT_target threshold. Telemetry data shall be logged and analyzed post-execution.
sub_frs: FR-2.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope.
FR-2.2: The teleoperation system shall not introduce latency that degrades cycle time beyond acceptable margin.
FR-2.3: The safety services shall not impose blocking delays that violate cycle time targets.
FR-2.4: Telemetry shall capture cycle time events (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end) with sufficient fidelity for p95 analysis.

candidate_dps: DP-2a: Design vehicle subsystems (hydraulics, drivetrain, bucket) for minimum cycle time through optimized actuation speeds, reduced dwell times, and parallel operations where feasible.
DP-2b: Implement predictive teleoperation with low-latency command transmission and local autonomy to minimize operator reaction time impact on cycle time.
DP-2c: Architect safety services as non-blocking, asynchronous monitors that flag hazards without halting cycle progression unless critical threshold breached.
DP-2d: Establish telemetry pipeline with sub-100ms event capture and edge-side cycle time computation to enable real-time performance feedback.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-2: Achieve cycle time target through integrated optimization across vehicle actuation performance (DP-2a), teleoperation latency reduction (DP-2b), non-blocking safety monitoring (DP-2c), and real-time telemetry instrumentation (DP-2d).

potential_sources: Vehicle actuation speed (DP-2a) is coupled to safety service response time (DP-2c): aggressive cycle timing may reduce safety margin if hazard detection/response is not sufficiently fast.
Teleoperation latency (DP-2b) is coupled to vehicle autonomy: if tele-operator commands are delayed, local autonomy must compensate, creating a hidden dependency on autonomous decision logic.
Telemetry fidelity (DP-2d) is coupled to cycle time measurement accuracy: if event timestamps are coarse, p95 computation may be unreliable, masking true performance.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP is a composite of four sub-DPs that interact. Coupling exists between safety and performance, and between teleoperation and autonomy. These couplings must be resolved at the next decomposition level or via explicit design rules (e.g., safety response time budget, teleoperation latency SLA, telemetry timestamp precision).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: CT_target is achievable within the physical constraints of the vehicle (hydraulic flow rates, actuator speeds, mechanical linkages).
Assumption: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, ambient temperature) remain within the agreed duty cycle envelope.
Risk: If teleoperation latency is not sufficiently reduced, operator-in-the-loop control may become the bottleneck, preventing cycle time target achievement.
Risk: If safety services are not architected as non-blocking, safety checks could serialize with cycle operations, adding unacceptable delay.
Risk: If telemetry event capture is not synchronized across subsystems, p95 computation may be inaccurate, leading to false acceptance/rejection of performance.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Decompose DP-2 into four independent sub-DPs (DP-2a, DP-2b, DP-2c, DP-2d) at the next level. Establish explicit coupling constraints: (1) Safety response time budget ≤ X ms; (2) Teleoperation latency SLA ≤ Y ms; (3) Telemetry timestamp precision ≤ Z ms. These constraints must be flowed down to subsystem design and verified via integration testing.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates vehicle performance, teleoperation, safety services, and telemetry to achieve the cycle time KPI. It owns the end-to-end cycle time performance contract and coordinates sub-module contributions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits cycle time events (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end) and computed p95 metrics to external monitoring/analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives cycle task commands (load, travel, dump) from site manager or tele-operator via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits safety alerts (hazard detections, threshold breaches) to Safety Manager and external safety systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link for teleoperation command/feedback exchange and telemetry data transmission.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of the cycle time KPI. It integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry export and external tasking/alert interfaces cross the boundary.

Vehicle owns actuation performance (DP-2a) but does not own teleoperation latency (DP-2b), safety service architecture (DP-2c), or telemetry instrumentation (DP-2d). Cycle time KPI is a system-level property, not a vehicle-only property. High diffusion: cycle time depends on external teleoperation and safety services.

Teleoperation owns latency reduction (DP-2b) but does not own vehicle actuation (DP-2a), safety services (DP-2c), or telemetry (DP-2d). Cycle time is not solely a teleoperation concern. High diffusion: cycle time depends on vehicle and safety subsystems.

Safety Services owns non-blocking safety monitoring (DP-2c) but does not own vehicle actuation, teleoperation, or telemetry. Cycle time is not a safety-primary concern; safety is a constraint on cycle time. Very high diffusion: cycle time is a system-level KPI, not a safety module responsibility.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of the cycle time KPI because it is the integration point for all four sub-DPs (vehicle actuation, teleoperation latency, safety services, telemetry). The cycle time target is a system-level performance contract that cannot be achieved by any single subsystem in isolation. NGHE Solution owns the end-to-end cycle time measurement, reporting, and performance accountability.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is assigned ownership but lacks explicit authority over Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services sub-module designs, the cycle time KPI may become a 'floating' requirement with no clear design driver. Mitigation: Establish explicit design rules and latency budgets that flow down from NGHE Solution to each sub-module.

Risk: If telemetry instrumentation is not fully contained within NGHE Solution or delegated to a single sub-module, cycle time measurement may be inconsistent or inaccurate. Mitigation: Assign telemetry event capture (DP-2d) to a dedicated sub-module (e.g., a telemetry/instrumentation service) that is owned by NGHE Solution.

Risk: If safety services are not architected as non-blocking, NGHE Solution may not be able to achieve cycle time targets without violating safety constraints. Mitigation: Establish a safety response time budget (e.g., ≤50 ms) that is flowed down to Safety Services and verified via integration testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign ownership of the cycle time KPI to NGHE Solution. Establish explicit sub-module design rules: (1) Vehicle shall achieve load→travel→dump sub-cycle times within X ms envelope; (2) Teleoperation shall introduce ≤Y ms latency; (3) Safety Services shall respond to hazards within ≤Z ms without blocking cycle progression; (4) Telemetry shall capture cycle events with ≤W ms timestamp precision. These rules must be flowed down to sub-module requirements and verified via integration testing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP-2 is a composite of four sub-DPs that exhibit coupling (safety vs. performance, teleoperation vs. autonomy, telemetry fidelity). The coupling is not fully resolved at this level. The DP must be decomposed further to establish explicit design rules and latency budgets that decouple the sub-DPs. Additionally, the DP does not explicitly address the duty cycle context or site conditions, which are part of the refined FR. Recommendation: Revise DP-2 to include explicit design rules for sub-module latency budgets and telemetry precision, or decompose DP-2 into four independent sub-DPs with clear coupling constraints.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Cycle time measurement (telemetry) may be diffused across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services if not explicitly owned by a single sub-module. Mitigation: Assign telemetry event capture to a dedicated instrumentation service owned by NGHE Solution.

Diffusion Risk 2: Cycle time performance accountability may be diffused if sub-modules do not have explicit latency budgets. Mitigation: Establish and flow down latency budgets from NGHE Solution to each sub-module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

Diffusion Risk 3: Safety constraint enforcement may be diffused if Safety Services is not architected as non-blocking. Mitigation: Establish a safety response time budget and verify that Safety Services can respond within this budget without blocking cycle progression.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate top-level owner of the cycle time KPI. It integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services and owns the end-to-end cycle time measurement and reporting. The assignment is sound, but diffusion risks must be mitigated via explicit sub-module design rules and latency budgets.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable via measurement of actual cycle time across ≥100 representative cycles under agreed duty cycle conditions. Telemetry data shall be logged and analyzed to compute p95 percentile. This verification approach is feasible and aligns with the NGHE Solution module's responsibility for telemetry instrumentation and reporting.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the cycle time KPI to NGHE Solution is clear. NGHE Solution owns the end-to-end cycle time performance contract and is responsible for coordinating sub-module contributions (vehicle actuation, teleoperation latency, safety services, telemetry) to achieve the target. Sub-module latency budgets must be established and flowed down to ensure clear allocation of responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1 (DP): Decompose DP-2 into four independent sub-DPs (DP-2a, DP-2b, DP-2c, DP-2d) at the next decomposition level. Establish explicit coupling constraints: (1) Safety response time budget ≤ X ms; (2) Teleoperation latency SLA ≤ Y ms; (3) Telemetry timestamp precision ≤ Z ms. These constraints must be flowed down to subsystem design and verified via integration testing.

Revision 2 (IM): Establish explicit sub-module design rules that flow down from NGHE Solution to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Each sub-module must have a clear latency budget and performance target that contributes to the overall cycle time KPI.

Revision 3 (FR): Add explicit acceptance criteria for duty cycle context (e.g., load mass range, terrain type, ambient temperature) to ensure that the cycle time target is achievable under the agreed conditions.

decision.justification: The refined FR is sound and verifiable. The selected DP-2 is a composite of four sub-DPs that exhibit coupling and must be decomposed further to establish explicit design rules and latency budgets. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and has high containment score (95). However, the coupling between sub-DPs (safety vs. performance, teleoperation vs. autonomy, telemetry fidelity) must be resolved at the next decomposition level. Proceeding to the next level will allow explicit design rules and latency budgets to be established, which will decouple the sub-DPs and enable clear allocation of responsibility to sub-modules.
decision.justification_detail: The FR-DP gate fails due to unresolved coupling in DP-2. The DP-IM gate passes, but diffusion risks are identified that must be mitigated via explicit sub-module design rules. The FR-IM gate passes, and verification is feasible. Overall, the decomposition is sound at the top level, but the next level must establish explicit coupling constraints and latency budgets to enable detailed design and verification.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: The vehicle shall execute load, travel, and dump sub-cycles with deterministic timing within design envelope (target: ≤X ms per sub-cycle).
FR-2.2: The teleoperation system shall introduce latency ≤Y ms from operator command to vehicle actuation, enabling responsive control without degrading cycle time.
FR-2.3: The safety services shall detect and respond to hazards within ≤Z ms without blocking cycle progression, maintaining safety while enabling cycle time targets.
FR-2.4: Telemetry shall capture cycle time events (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end) with timestamp precision ≤W ms, enabling accurate p95 analysis.

assumptions: Assumption 1: CT_target is achievable within the physical constraints of the vehicle (hydraulic flow rates, actuator speeds, mechanical linkages).
Assumption 2: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, ambient temperature) remain within the agreed duty cycle envelope.
Assumption 3: Teleoperation latency can be reduced to ≤Y ms through optimized command transmission and local autonomy.
Assumption 4: Safety services can be architected as non-blocking, responding to hazards within ≤Z ms without halting cycle progression.
Assumption 5: Telemetry event capture can be synchronized across subsystems with timestamp precision ≤W ms.
open_risks: Risk 1: If teleoperation latency is not sufficiently reduced, operator-in-the-loop control may become the bottleneck, preventing cycle time target achievement.
Risk 2: If safety services are not architected as non-blocking, safety checks could serialize with cycle operations, adding unacceptable delay.
Risk 3: If telemetry event capture is not synchronized across subsystems, p95 computation may be inaccurate, leading to false acceptance/rejection of performance.
Risk 4: If sub-module latency budgets are not established and flowed down, cycle time performance accountability may be diffused across multiple modules.
Risk 5: If duty cycle context (load mass, terrain, ambient temperature) is not explicitly defined, cycle time target may be unachievable under actual site conditions.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Vehicle actuation performance data (load→travel→dump sub-cycle times) under agreed duty cycle conditions.
Evidence 2: Teleoperation latency measurements (command transmission, feedback, local autonomy response times) under representative network conditions.
Evidence 3: Safety services response time measurements (hazard detection, alert generation, response blocking) under representative hazard scenarios.
Evidence 4: Telemetry event capture and timestamp precision validation across subsystems.
Evidence 5: Integration test results demonstrating p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle conditions.
Evidence 6: Design documentation establishing explicit sub-module latency budgets and coupling constraints.
